/blog/design-on-autopilot-how-lovdes-tur.title: Design on Autopilot: How Lovdes Turns a Sentence into Finished Figma Layouts - Hi Thuc
/blog/design-on-autopilot-how-lovdes-tur.description: Launch faster. Spend less. Convert more. I design and build AI-powered web products end-to-end, so you go from idea to a live, revenue-ready product without the agency price tag or the wait.
h1: Design on Autopilot: How Lovdes Turns a Sentence into Finished Figma Layouts
h2: The contradiction at the heart of "automated design"
h3: From Idea to Live Website in Weeks: How a Solo Builder Ships Like a Whole Team
Landing Pages That Sell While You Sleep: Lessons from Building 4 Product Sites
Turn a Silent Website into a Talking Salesperson: Inside Sellvoxa
One AI Agent, Three Brains (Claude, Gemini, GPT): Picking the Right Model for the Job
p: Hi Thức
A closer look at automating creative work without gutting it — how Lovdes turns a described idea into editable, native Figma layers, and the principle that keeps the designer firmly in control.
7 min read

Design is judgment, and judgment automates badly. So "AI does the design" was never the goal — it's a contradiction that produces generic, lifeless work. The honest goal for Lovdes is narrower and more useful: automate the mechanical scaffolding, and hand the human a real draft to react to. The taste stays where it belongs — with the person.

Here's the distinction most AI design tools get wrong. Their output is a flat image — pretty in a demo, useless in practice, because you can't *finish* it. To change anything, you'd rebuild it by hand from scratch. That's not a starting point; it's a screenshot.

Lovdes works differently, and the difference is the entire value. It builds the layout as semantic HTML first — structured and predictable — then renders it as *native Figma nodes*. Real layers. You can grab them, restyle them, move them, and complete the work the normal way. The output isn't a picture of a design; it's a design you can keep working on. A draft you can edit beats a polished image you have to throw away, every time.

The way I think about it: the machine does the tedious 70% — the structure, the spacing, a sane first hierarchy, placeholder content to react to. The designer spends their time on the 30% that genuinely needs taste: the judgment calls a model can't and shouldn't make for you.

The point was never to remove the designer. It's to delete the part of their day that was never creative in the first place — the blank-canvas setup, the boilerplate, the fiddly arranging — so their attention goes to the decisions only they can make.

One more thing that sounds minor and isn't: Lovdes runs *inside* Figma and Photoshop. No exporting, no tab-switching, no copy-paste seam between "the AI tool" and "my real tool." The draft appears where you already work. Automation that makes you leave your tool to use it quietly gives back much of the time it claims to save. The flow has to be unbroken, or it isn't a workflow improvement at all.

This is how I think about creative automation in general, not just design: generate the scaffolding, keep the control. Hand the human something real to refine, and never pretend to replace the part only they can do. Get that line right and automation feels like leverage. Get it wrong — automate the judgment instead of the busywork — and it feels like a downgrade with extra steps.
(Lovdes is still being finished, with early access opening in 2026, so this is the approach I'm building on — not a promise of magic.)
*Automating the scaffolding is how a small team launches faster and spends less — without losing the human touch.*

/blog/building-an-ai-mobile-app-people-a.title: Building an AI Mobile App People Actually Use: The Aimatee Journey to Google Play - Hi Thuc
/blog/building-an-ai-mobile-app-people-a.description: Launch faster. Spend less. Convert more. I design and build AI-powered web products end-to-end, so you go from idea to a live, revenue-ready product without the agency price tag or the wait.
h1: Building an AI Mobile App People Actually Use: The Aimatee Journey to Google Play
h2: The part that has nothing to do with code
h3: From Idea to Live Website in Weeks: How a Solo Builder Ships Like a Whole Team
Landing Pages That Sell While You Sleep: Lessons from Building 4 Product Sites
Turn a Silent Website into a Talking Salesperson: Inside Sellvoxa
One AI Agent, Three Brains (Claude, Gemini, GPT): Picking the Right Model for the Job
p: Hi Thức

An honest, story-driven account of taking Aimatee from idea to a live Android app — the one problem I refused to broaden, the design decisions that focus forced, and the unglamorous reality of getting onto the Play Store alone.
8 min read

Picture someone who reads English technical docs without trouble and writes perfectly decent emails. Then a meeting starts, the floor turns to them, and their throat closes. The English they "know" evaporates the instant they have to *speak* it, live, in front of people. Years of study, and that one moment still beats them.

That moment — common, quiet, and a little humiliating — is the entire reason Aimatee exists. Not "people want to learn English." Something far more specific: capable professionals who can't get the words out when it counts.

The default move for a solo builder is, ironically, to think too big. "An English-learning app" sounds like the goal. It's also a swamp — generic, infinite in scope, and competing head-on with companies that have hundreds of engineers.

The discipline was to refuse that version. No vocabulary decks. No grammar courses. One job: practice *speaking* in the situations that actually scare you, until they stop scaring you. Every time scope tried to creep — "we could also add reading," "what about IELTS prep" — the answer was no. Focus wasn't a constraint I tolerated; it was the product.

Once the problem was that narrow, it chose the features for me. Instead of abstract lessons, Aimatee mirrors the three things people actually do at work: messaging, voice calls, and meetings. You practice in the shape of the real thing.

And instead of a faceless tutor, there's a crew of AI colleagues — each with a role, a personality, and memory. The memory is the point, not a gimmick. The fear is *social*, so the practice has to feel social and continuous, not like a quiz that resets to zero each time. A colleague who remembers last week's conversation makes the practice feel like a relationship, and relationships are what lower the stakes. Best of all, they don't judge. You can be bad, out loud, repeatedly, in complete privacy — which is exactly the safety people need before they'll risk it with a real human.

Here's what no one warns you about: shipping to a store is its own job, and it barely touches programming. Store listing and screenshots. Policy and data-safety compliance. App signing. Release tracks. The review wait that you can't speed up. Solo, every bit of that lands on you, and it's easy to underestimate by weeks.

It's unglamorous and nobody claps for it. But it's the entire difference between "I built something" and "people can install it." I'd argue it's where a lot of solo projects quietly die — not in the building, but in the last mile to the store.

No varnish: Aimatee is live on Android, free to start, with Pro and Career tiers for people who want more. iOS is still ahead of me, not behind — it isn't done. I made the deliberate call to ship one platform that works rather than demo two that don't.
A real app with real users teaches you more in a week than a perfect roadmap does in a quarter. That trade — coherence and shipping over breadth — is the one I'd make again.
*Shipping one focused platform that works is how you launch faster — and hear from real users sooner.*

/blog/automating-the-boring-stuff-how-i-.title: Automating the Boring Stuff: How I Let AI Handle 80% of Repetitive Work - Hi Thuc
/blog/automating-the-boring-stuff-how-i-.description: Launch faster. Spend less. Convert more. I design and build AI-powered web products end-to-end, so you go from idea to a live, revenue-ready product without the agency price tag or the wait.
h1: Automating the Boring Stuff: How I Let AI Handle 80% of Repetitive Work
h2: Automation is oversold; the useful version is small
What makes a task a good candidate
h3: From Idea to Live Website in Weeks: How a Solo Builder Ships Like a Whole Team
Landing Pages That Sell While You Sleep: Lessons from Building 4 Product Sites
Turn a Silent Website into a Talking Salesperson: Inside Sellvoxa
One AI Agent, Three Brains (Claude, Gemini, GPT): Picking the Right Model for the Job
p: Hi Thức
A grounded take on workflow automation: the difference between the steps worth automating and the ones you should never touch, told mainly through the invisible plumbing that keeps Peachgen running.
6 min read

Most automation pitches promise a business that runs itself. The honest, valuable version is much narrower: find the steps a human repeats *identically* every single time, and hand those to software. The win isn't replacing anyone's judgment. It's reclaiming hours from work that never needed a human in the first place.
I'll use Peachgen to make this concrete, because the best automation in it is the part users never see.

Peachgen runs on credits and payments. When a customer pays, credits need to appear in their account — instantly, correctly, every time, without me watching. That's not one action; it's a chain: payment confirmation comes in, gets verified, the balance updates, the customer sees it. Done by hand, that chain is slow, error-prone, and — fatally — it doesn't work at 2 a.m. Automated, it's a non-event. Nobody thinks about it, which is the goal.

The same logic runs through the product. Generations get queued and processed in order. Every result is saved to history automatically, so nothing is lost. Multi-step workflows are chained so a single click does what used to take five separate ones. None of this is flashy. All of it is hours and mistakes removed from the system, quietly, forever.
After enough of this, the pattern is easy to spot. A good automation candidate is:
- **High frequency** — you do it often enough that the savings compound.
- **Rule-based** — the same inputs always produce the same right action.
- **Clear at the edges** — defined inputs, defined outputs, no ambiguity in between.
- **Painful by hand** — tedious, and easy to get wrong when tired.

Onboarding emails, report generation, syncing data between two tools, formatting and publishing content. The tell is that a good candidate is *boring*. If a task is boring and predictable, that's exactly what makes it safe to hand off.

The line I hold: judgment calls stay with people. Anything where the right answer depends on context, taste, or a relationship should not be handed to a script. What you automate are the steps *around* the decision — gathering the data, formatting it, routing it to the right person — so the human spends their attention on the call itself, not the busywork surrounding it.
Automation that pretends to have judgment is precisely where automation earns its bad name. The skill isn't automating as much as possible. It's knowing exactly where to stop.

You don't need fancy software to start. You need to notice the thing you do every Tuesday that you quietly resent, and ask one question: does it follow rules? Most offices are full of these — small, repeated, rule-shaped chores nobody chose to keep. Each one you remove is compounding time handed back to the work that actually matters.
*Automating the rule-shaped work is the most reliable way to spend less without cutting anything that matters.*

/blog/turn-a-silent-website-into-a-talki.title: Turn a Silent Website into a Talking Salesperson: Inside Sellvoxa - Hi Thuc
/blog/turn-a-silent-website-into-a-talki.description: Launch faster. Spend less. Convert more. I design and build AI-powered web products end-to-end, so you go from idea to a live, revenue-ready product without the agency price tag or the wait.
h1: Turn a Silent Website into a Talking Salesperson: Inside Sellvoxa
h2: The insight: most websites are monologues
"One line of script" is a product decision, not a convenience
Learning the business without making things up
An agent that can act needs brakes
Where it fits — and where it doesn't
h3: From Idea to Live Website in Weeks: How a Solo Builder Ships Like a Whole Team
Landing Pages That Sell While You Sleep: Lessons from Building 4 Product Sites
One AI Agent, Three Brains (Claude, Gemini, GPT): Picking the Right Model for the Job
Building an AI Mobile App People Actually Use: The Aimatee Journey to Google Play
p: Hi Thức

A deeper look at Sellvoxa: the insight behind a website that speaks first, why "one line of script" is a product decision rather than a convenience, and the guardrails that make an agent which can *act* on your site safe enough to trust.
5 min read

A website talks *at* you. It presents, and then it waits — for you to read everything, understand it, and decide on your own. The visitor who has a question right now has nowhere to put it at the exact moment they have it. So they leave, and you never know they were close.

That gap — between *has a question* and *gets an answer* — is where a lot of sales quietly die. Sellvoxa is built to close it in the moment, by voice, the way a good shop assistant would: noticing you're interested, and speaking first.
The thing Sellvoxa is embedded with — a single `<script>` tag, no backend, no code changes — looks like a marketing line. It's actually the hardest and most important constraint in the whole product.

It means the customer needs no engineering team, no server, and no rebuild. It has to work whether their site is on WordPress, Wix, Shopify, or hand-rolled code. Every feature I add has to survive that constraint: if it can't ship inside one embeddable script, it doesn't ship. That discipline is exactly what separates "a project you have to integrate" from "paste it and it works." Most of the engineering is in the part the customer never sees — making that simplicity true.
The failure mode of every sales AI is the confident lie: inventing a price, a policy, a feature that doesn't exist. One bad hallucination about a refund can cost more than the tool ever earns.

So Sellvoxa grounds itself in the customer's own material. It reads their pages, products, and documents to build its knowledge, and answers from that — not from the open-ended guesses of a general model. You can update that knowledge anytime, and the agent follows. It's an honest trade: the assistant is exactly as good as the content you give it. Thin content, thin answers. That's a feature, not a bug — it keeps the agent tethered to what's actually true about your business.

Sellvoxa doesn't only talk. It can do things on the page — scroll to a section, open the right page, fill in a form to move a visitor toward the cart or a sign-up. That's where it gets genuinely useful, and genuinely risky. Anything that can act on your site can also act *wrongly*.

So control isn't an afterthought; it's part of the design. Every permission is a toggle you own. Filling a form never auto-submits it. You can cap the cost of a single session. The point is that a business should be able to hand this agent the keys to its storefront and still sleep at night. Power without brakes isn't a product you can responsibly sell.

I'll be straight about the boundary. If your site is pure self-serve — documentation, a simple download — a talking agent is overhead nobody asked for. But if your business has to advise, explain, or persuade before someone buys — courses, services, considered purchases — then a site that speaks first genuinely changes how visitors behave. Confident enough in that to eat my own cooking: Sellvoxa's own agent runs live on Sellvoxa's site.
*A website that speaks first is one of the most direct ways to convert more from the traffic you already have.*

/blog/web-app-or-mobile-app-an-honest-gu.title: Web App or Mobile App? An Honest Guide for Founders - Hi Thuc
/blog/web-app-or-mobile-app-an-honest-gu.description: Launch faster. Spend less. Convert more. I design and build AI-powered web products end-to-end, so you go from idea to a live, revenue-ready product without the agency price tag or the wait.
h1: Web App or Mobile App? An Honest Guide for Founders
h2: "I need an app" is usually a guess
The true cost of native — the part nobody quotes you
The one question that usually settles it
When web is the honest answer
h3: From Idea to Live Website in Weeks: How a Solo Builder Ships Like a Whole Team
Landing Pages That Sell While You Sleep: Lessons from Building 4 Product Sites
Turn a Silent Website into a Talking Salesperson: Inside Sellvoxa
One AI Agent, Three Brains (Claude, Gemini, GPT): Picking the Right Model for the Job
p: Hi Thức

A straight, experience-based guide to one of the most expensive early decisions a founder makes — native app versus web — including the costs nobody quotes you and the single question that usually settles it.
6 min read

It's one of the most common things a client opens with. And often, "app" is a stand-in for something else: *I want to look serious*, or *a competitor has one*. Neither is a reason to build native. The decision deserves more than instinct, because it's one of the hardest early calls to reverse once you've committed.
When people compare web and native, they compare build cost. That's the small part. The ongoing taxes are what hurt:
- **Slower iteration.** Every change waits on an app-store review you don't control. Web ships a fix in minutes; native ships it when someone else approves it.
- **Two platforms, or a hard choice.** iOS and Android double the surface, or force you to abandon half your market on day one.
- **The install wall.** A user has to *decide* to download — and commit storage and trust — before they get any value at all. That's a real conversion cliff that web simply doesn't have.
None of these are one-time costs. They're rent you pay for the life of the product.
Here's the test I actually use: *does this need to live in someone's pocket and use the phone itself?*

Aimatee is a mobile app because the answer is an obvious yes. It's daily speaking practice. It uses the microphone for real voice calls. It leans on push notifications to build a habit. It's the kind of thing you open for a few minutes between tasks. The phone isn't a delivery channel for Aimatee — the phone *is* the product surface. Strip it out and the product stops making sense.

If usage is occasional, task-based, or tied to a desktop workflow, web is almost always right. It runs where the work already happens, updates instantly, and has no install barrier. A lot of products that ask to be "apps" are really web tools wearing a store icon — and forcing them native adds friction while removing nothing they needed.

The pragmatic path for most founders: validate on web first. Prove people want it and will actually use it, while you can still change everything cheaply and ship daily. Then go native *once* the habit is real and the device features (camera, offline, notifications) genuinely justify the cost.

The wrong choice here isn't fatal — but the right one saves months. I'd rather talk a client out of a build they don't need than write the bigger invoice. That's not charity; it's how you end up with a client who trusts you for the next ten years instead of one.
*Choosing the right platform up front is the simplest way to spend less and launch faster.*

/blog/one-ai-agent-three-brains.title: One AI Agent, Three Brains (Claude, Gemini, GPT): Picking the Right Model for the Job - Hi Thuc
/blog/one-ai-agent-three-brains.description: Launch faster. Spend less. Convert more. I design and build AI-powered web products end-to-end, so you go from idea to a live, revenue-ready product without the agency price tag or the wait.
h1: One AI Agent, Three Brains (Claude, Gemini, GPT): Picking the Right Model for the Job
h2: The trap of the one true model
The failure modes it buys you out of
h3: From Idea to Live Website in Weeks: How a Solo Builder Ships Like a Whole Team
Landing Pages That Sell While You Sleep: Lessons from Building 4 Product Sites
Turn a Silent Website into a Talking Salesperson: Inside Sellvoxa
Building an AI Mobile App People Actually Use: The Aimatee Journey to Google Play
p: Hi Thức

A practical, honest look at building a multi-model AI agent: why no model wins every task, what routing actually means, the failure modes it buys you out of, and the engineering tax that comes with it.
5 min read

It's tempting to standardize on whichever model is best *today* and build everything around it. The problem is that "best today" is a depreciating asset. The leaderboard reshuffles every few months; prices move; a new version can quietly get worse at the one thing you depended on. Couple your whole product to a single model's quirks and you've tied yourself to a moving target you don't control.
Lovdes — my AI design assistant for Figma and Photoshop — takes the other path. It runs across Claude, Gemini, and GPT, and chooses the right one for each task.
The key realization is that a design assistant isn't doing one job. It's doing several, and they reward different things:
- **Turning a vague idea into a sensible layout hierarchy** is a reasoning problem — it rewards a model that's strong at structure and intent.
- **Small, high-frequency edits** ("make this tighter," "swap these two") reward speed and low cost, because there are many of them.
- **Writing natural placeholder copy** rewards yet another strength.
No single model is reliably top of all three. Hard-coding one means quietly losing on the tasks it's weak at. Routing means each step goes to whatever handles it best.

It isn't magic; it's a layer. A request is classified by task type, sent to the model best suited to it, and returned through a normalized interface so the rest of the system doesn't care who answered. Crucially, the user never sees any of it. They describe the outcome they want; the agent picks the model. No model selector, no prompt-tuning homework dumped on a designer who just wanted a layout.
Designing for multiple models isn't only about quality per task. It's insurance:
- **A provider outage** doesn't take your product down — reroute.
- **A price jump** on one provider doesn't wreck your margins — move the cost-sensitive tasks.
- **A model regression** — a new version that's worse at your task — isn't a crisis; pin the old one or route around it.
A single-model build has none of these escape hatches. When its one provider has a bad day, so do you.

This is not free, and pretending otherwise would be dishonest. Multiple models mean more integrations, more credentials to manage, more to monitor, and prompts that behave differently across providers — so you can't always write once and run everywhere. There's a real complexity tax.

For a weekend tool, that tax isn't worth it: pick one model and move on. But for an agent meant to live for years inside someone's daily workflow, designing to swap models is the cheaper choice over the long run. (Lovdes is still pre-launch, with early access opening in 2026 — so this is the architecture I'm building on, not a victory lap.)
*Building to swap models is also how you spend less over time — no costly rewrite each time the model landscape shifts.*

/blog/landing-pages-that-sell-while-you-.title: Landing Pages That Sell While You Sleep: Lessons from Building 4 Product Sites - Hi Thuc
/blog/landing-pages-that-sell-while-you-.description: Launch faster. Spend less. Convert more. I design and build AI-powered web products end-to-end, so you go from idea to a live, revenue-ready product without the agency price tag or the wait.
h1: Landing Pages That Sell While You Sleep: Lessons from Building 4 Product Sites
h2: Lesson 1 — Lead with the outcome, not the feature (Sellvoxa)
Lesson 2 — Name the fear, not the category (Aimatee)
h3: From Idea to Live Website in Weeks: How a Solo Builder Ships Like a Whole Team
Turn a Silent Website into a Talking Salesperson: Inside Sellvoxa
One AI Agent, Three Brains (Claude, Gemini, GPT): Picking the Right Model for the Job
Building an AI Mobile App People Actually Use: The Aimatee Journey to Google Play
p: Hi Thức
The patterns that consistently separate a landing page that converts from a pretty one that doesn't — each illustrated by a single project, so the lesson stays clear.
8 min read

A landing page has exactly one job: help a visitor understand what you do and take one next step. Everything else is decoration. Building four product sites taught me four things about doing that one job well. I'll give each lesson a single example, because a principle is easier to trust when you can see it work in one clean case.
The Sellvoxa page opens with a pain, not a product: an ordinary website just sits and waits while customers slip away. Only after that does it introduce voice AI as the answer.

The reason is human. A visitor doesn't care about your technology until they feel the problem is *theirs*. A feature is an answer to a question — and if you haven't made them ask the question yet, the answer lands on deaf ears. Naming the pain first is what turns "neat tech" into "I need this." Lead with the wound, then show the bandage.

Aimatee could have introduced itself as "an English-learning app." It doesn't. It leads with a specific, slightly uncomfortable feeling: studying English for years and still freezing the moment you have to speak at work.

The category — language learning — is crowded and abstract. The fear is specific and yours. When you describe the exact moment someone is stuck in, they feel seen, and "seen" is what earns the next scroll. Talk to the person in the chair, not to the market on a slide.
Lovdes is still pre-launch, and its site does one disciplined thing: it funnels everything toward leaving an email for early access. No competing buttons, no five-way menu of things to do.

This is harder than it sounds, because every stakeholder instinct says "while they're here, let's also…". But a page that asks for five things gets none of them done. Conversion is subtraction. The most useful word in landing-page design is *no* — to the extra CTA, the extra section, the clever idea that dilutes the single ask.

Peachgen has a lot to show — image, video, and voice generation all in one place. The temptation with a rich product is to dump everything on the first screen. Instead, the page is built as a staircase: each section earns the next. Show the outcome, then the capability, then proof from the community's own work, then the call to start.

Structure as a staircase, not a pile. A visitor will climb if each step is worth it; they'll bounce off a wall of everything-at-once. And underneath all four lessons sit two non-negotiables that no amount of cleverness replaces: it loads fast, and it reads well on a phone.

If there's one idea connecting all four, it's restraint. A landing page that does one thing clearly will out-convert a beautiful one that tries to do everything. The craft isn't adding — it's deciding what to leave out.
*This is how you convert more without spending more — the gains come from restraint, not budget.*

/blog/from-idea-to-live.title: From Idea to Live Website in Weeks: How a Solo Builder Ships Like a Whole Team - Hi Thuc
/blog/from-idea-to-live.description: Launch faster. Spend less. Convert more. I design and build AI-powered web products end-to-end, so you go from idea to a live, revenue-ready product without the agency price tag or the wait.
h1: From Idea to Live Website in Weeks: How a Solo Builder Ships Like a Whole Team
h2: Start with the sentence, not the screen
A stack I no longer have to think about
Design and code as one loop

Here's the part that genuinely saves weeks: I don't design a perfect mockup and then "implement" it. I design *in* the medium. A rough layout goes straight into code, into the browser, onto a real phone, with real copy — never lorem ipsum. Then I react to what I actually see.
Where solo is a trade-off — honestly
h3: Landing Pages That Sell While You Sleep: Lessons from Building 4 Product Sites
Turn a Silent Website into a Talking Salesperson: Inside Sellvoxa
One AI Agent, Three Brains (Claude, Gemini, GPT): Picking the Right Model for the Job
Building an AI Mobile App People Actually Use: The Aimatee Journey to Google Play
p: Hi Thức

A behind-the-scenes look at how I take a product from a rough idea to a live marketing site in a matter of weeks, using the build of the Sellvoxa landing page as a single worked example — from the first sentence down to deploy.
7 min read

When a website slips from "a few weeks" to "a few months," it's rarely the code that did it. It's the gaps between people. The copywriter is waiting on the brand brief. The designer is waiting on final copy. The developer is waiting on the final design. Each handoff adds a queue, a meeting, and a round of "actually, can we change…". The real hours of work might be small; the calendar time is enormous.

Working solo doesn't make me faster at any one of those crafts than a specialist. What it removes is the queue. When the same person writes the headline, decides the layout, and builds it, a change that would normally cost a two-day round-trip costs ten minutes. That compounding is the whole trick — and it's why I'll walk through one project, the Sellvoxa landing page, instead of listing many. The loop is easier to see on a single site.

Before I open a design tool, I try to write the product in one sentence a stranger would understand. For Sellvoxa it became: *a voice assistant you add to any website with one line of code, that talks to your visitors and helps them buy.*

That sentence does a surprising amount of work. It tells me the hero has to lead with the *problem* — a website that just sits there and waits — and resolve it with the *outcome* — a site that talks and sells. Not with the technology. If I can't write the sentence cleanly, the site isn't ready to design; the thinking isn't finished. Most "design problems" I hit later turn out to be unfinished-thinking problems wearing a costume.

I build every site on the same foundation: React with Tailwind on the front end, deployed on Cloudflare. This is deliberate, and a little boring. Because I'm not re-evaluating tools each project, the entire mechanical layer — components, spacing scale, deploy pipeline — is muscle memory. I spend my limited decision-making budget on what's actually different about *this* product, not on how to center a div or wire up a form.

Reuse also means quality compounds. The form validation I got right once, the scroll behavior, the responsive breakpoints that actually hold up on a real phone — they carry forward. Each site starts further along than the last one did.

This matters because a landing page lies in a static mockup. Spacing that looks elegant in a design file feels cramped when you thumb-scroll it on a phone at arm's length. A headline that fit the artboard wraps badly on a narrow screen. Seeing the real thing early kills a whole category of late surprises. By the time the page "looks done," it *is* done — it's already the live artifact, not a picture of one waiting to be rebuilt.

This isn't free, and I won't pretend it is. A solo build has no second pair of eyes on the blind spot, no specialist to push the visual craft past my own ceiling, and a genuine bus-factor of one. For a large product with many surfaces and a long lifespan, that's a real liability, and I'll say so to a client's face.

But for the job most founders actually have — get a clear, fast, credible site live so you can start talking to the market — one accountable person who can move from sentence to deploy without a relay race is usually the right tool. Speed without a committee, and a site that's coherent because a single mind carried it the whole way.
*This is what "launch faster" actually looks like in practice — one accountable person, one clean line from idea to deploy.*

/work/sellvoxa.title: Sell Voxa - Hi Thuc
/work/sellvoxa.description: Sellvoxa is an embeddable voice AI agent that turns any website into a proactive sales rep, built for businesses and sellers who want their site to greet, advise, and convert — with a single line of script.
h2:
A Website That Speaks First,
Designed to Convert
A Channel That Works Around the Clock
Designed to Engage, Built to Sell
h3: Peach Gen
Aimatee
p: Hi Thức
Category
Voice AI · Conversational AI · Web Widget · Sales/Conversion SaaS
Year
Live Link

Sellvoxa is an embeddable voice AI agent that turns any website into a proactive sales rep, built for businesses and sellers who want their site to greet, advise, and convert — with a single line of script.

Sellvoxa — selling through voice — is an agent crafted for websites that should do more than wait. Designed for sellers, service businesses, and online stores, it gives a static page a voice, a presence, and the instinct to speak first.
Built for teams who don't have engineers to spare, Sellvoxa drops in with one line of script and starts working from the very first visitor.

Sellvoxa was conceived as an agent that leads the conversation rather than waiting to be asked. Every interaction is intentional — greeting at the right moment, answering in real time, and guiding toward action — balancing a natural, human feel with full brand control.
Sellvoxa needs no technical team. Paste a single `<script>` line into your page — no source-code changes, no backend — and the assistant learns your entire website, then begins to talk.
It reads your pages, products, and documents to understand your business, then builds a proactive conversation script that fits, answering only from your own knowledge — which you can update anytime.

Sellvoxa talks by voice in real time, with low latency that feels like speaking to a real consultant. It listens, replies, and detects your visitor's language automatically — Vietnamese, English, and more — switching between them mid-conversation without a pause.
Instead of waiting to be questioned, it greets visitors at the right moment, with a tailored opening for each page.

Sellvoxa doesn't just answer — it moves people forward. It scrolls the page, opens the right section, and fills in forms (only when permitted) to carry visitors to the cart, the buy button, or the sign-up form.
You stay fully in control: toggle each permission, set per-session cost limits, and manage knowledge — transparent and brand-safe by design.
Sellvoxa doesn't just make a strong first impression — it never clocks out. Serving 24/7, it captures the after-hours visitors a passive site would lose, converting from the traffic you already have.

Every part of Sellvoxa works toward one outcome: a website that serves and persuades on its own. It gives businesses a proactive voice while keeping them in command — the result is a site that feels alive, helpful, and ready to close. A Hithuc original.

/work/lovdes.title: Lovdes - Hi Thuc
/work/lovdes.description: Lovdes is an AI design assistant that lives inside Figma and Photoshop, built for designers who want to move from idea to finished layout in seconds — commanding their tools in natural language.
h2:
A Partner That Listens,
Designed to Build With You
A Tool That Grows With You
h3: Peach Gen
Aimatee
p: Hi Thức
Category
AI Design Tool · Figma/Photoshop Plugin · Productivity SaaS (Pre-launch / Waitlist)
Year
Live Link
Lovdes is an AI design assistant that lives inside Figma and Photoshop, built for designers who want to move from idea to finished layout in seconds — commanding their tools in natural language.

Lovdes — love and design, joined in one word — is an assistant crafted for designers who want their tools to keep pace with their thinking. Designed for Vietnamese designers, studios, and product teams, it turns a spoken idea into real, editable design — directly on the canvas you already work in.
Built for creators who don't want to wrestle with repetitive work, Lovdes offers a partner that feels native to Figma and Photoshop from the very first click.

Lovdes was conceived as an assistant that creates rather than suggests. Every interaction is intentional — you describe, it builds, and the result lands as native layers — balancing the freedom of conversation with the precision of structured design.

Lovdes runs directly within Figma and Photoshop — no tab-switching, no copy-paste, no broken flow. Install it in a single click, describe what you want in natural Vietnamese, and watch the design take shape on your canvas.
It builds full layouts in semantic HTML, then renders them as native Figma nodes — so everything stays clean, structured, and ready to refine by hand.
Lovdes is multi-model by design. Claude, Gemini, and GPT work together behind the scenes, with the system automatically choosing the best one for each task — layout, copy, or fine adjustment.
You never manage models or wrangle prompts; you simply describe the outcome, and Lovdes orchestrates the rest on Cloudflare's AI infrastructure.
Lovdes is Vietnamese-first at its core. Command and converse in natural Vietnamese, with a workflow shaped around how local designers actually work.
Figma and Photoshop arrive at launch, with Illustrator next on the roadmap, and Sketch support and JSON design-token export already shaped by early community requests.

Lovdes doesn't just speed up one project — it reshapes a daily workflow. As your design needs expand, the assistant keeps learning new skills and surfaces, supporting bigger ideas without adding friction.

Every part of Lovdes works toward one goal: design that keeps pace with thought. Now in its final polishing phase with a free early-access waitlist, it's built to help creators design faster and smarter — right inside the tools they already love. A Hithuc original.

/work/aimatee.title: Aimatee - Hi Thuc
/work/aimatee.description: Aimatee is a mobile app for learning workplace English through Contextual Learning, built for Vietnamese professionals who want to practice speaking — messaging, calling, and meeting — alongside a crew of AI colleagues.
h2:
A Workplace to Practice In,
Designed to Build Confidence
Designed to Immerse, Built to Prepare
h3: Peach Gen
Lovdes
p: Hi Thức
Category
EdTech · AI Language Learning · Mobile App
Year
Live Link

Aimatee is a mobile app for learning workplace English through Contextual Learning, built for Vietnamese professionals who want to practice speaking — messaging, calling, and meeting — alongside a crew of AI colleagues.

Aimatee — your AI mate at work — is an app crafted for people who've studied English for years yet still freeze when it's time to speak. Designed for Vietnamese tech workers and professionals, it builds a simulated workplace where every conversation is practice and every colleague is patient.
Built for learners who don't want another lesson, Aimatee offers a living environment that feels like a real job from day one.

Aimatee was conceived as an app that immerses rather than instructs. Every scenario is intentional — set in your own role and career path — so the English you practice is the English you'll actually use, balancing real pressure with a space that never judges.

Aimatee mirrors the office day across three channels. Message one-on-one and in group channels, take real voice calls with in-call feedback on pronunciation and vocabulary, and join multi-person meetings — stand-ups, reviews, planning — to build reflexes while the room listens.
Beyond the daily flow, career scenarios — interviews, salary negotiations, applying for a new role — get you ready for the moments that matter most.

Aimatee surrounds you with six AI colleagues, each with a clear role and personality — from Design Lead to UX Researcher. They remember your goals, your projects, and your past conversations, so every session continues the last.
After each exchange, instant "Better way / Better reply" suggestions help you phrase things more naturally — correction without embarrassment, growth without fear.
Aimatee is designed to start in minutes. Create your seat with your role and level, meet your crew, practice daily, and watch your progress as conversations grow more natural week by week.
Free to begin and available on Android, with Pro and Career plans for those who want to practice more — and iOS on the way.
Aimatee doesn't just teach a lesson — it grows alongside you. As your English and your career advance, the crew adapts, keeping conversations sharp and relevant to the work you actually do.

Every part of Aimatee works toward one promise: confidence before the real conversation. It lets learners make mistakes freely while steadily building fluency — so by the time you face an interview, a negotiation, or a new job, you're ready to speak. A Hithuc original.

/work/peachgen.title: Peach Gen - Hi Thuc
/work/peachgen.description: Peachgen is a bold, all-in-one AI creative platform, built for marketers, creators, and small teams who want studio-grade images, videos, and voices without studio-grade effort.
h2: Speed That Feels Like Magic, Quality That Looks Deliberate
A Studio That Grows With You
Designed to Create, Built to Empower
h3: Aimatee
Lovdes
p: Hi Thức
Category
Generative AI · Creative SaaS · AI Image/Video/Voice
Year
Live Link
Peachgen is a bold, all-in-one AI creative platform, built for marketers, creators, and small teams who want studio-grade images, videos, and voices without studio-grade effort.

Peachgen — where a "peachy" idea becomes a finished creation — is a platform crafted for people who think in pictures, not in toolbars. Designed for marketers, content creators, and modern product teams, it turns a single line of description into images, videos, and voices that look professionally made.
Built for creators who don't want to start from a blank canvas, Peachgen offers a foundation that feels fast and alive from the very first prompt.

Peachgen was conceived as a platform that removes the gap between idea and output. Every tool is intentional — guiding you from a thought to a 4K result in thirty seconds, balancing raw creative speed with the polish of a professional finish.

Peachgen brings image, video, and voice generation under one roof. Describe a scene and get art; feed in a prompt or a still and get a cinematic clip; type a script and hear it spoken in a natural voice — or cloned in your own.
Soon, a region-based photo editor will let you paint over any area, describe the change, and watch it happen — three independent brush layers, professional control, no software to learn.

Peachgen is built to keep you moving. An intuitive canvas, a rich library of prompt templates organized by use case, and a community showcase of the platform's best work — over 2.5 million creations and counting — keep inspiration one click away.
What used to take three hours with traditional tools now takes thirty seconds, so creating becomes a habit rather than a project.
Peachgen is designed with flexibility at its core. A transparent credits model — ten free to start, from just 2,000₫ — Google sign-in, and automatic payments mean you only pay for what you make.
Workflows and super-workflows let power users chain steps together, while every creation is saved to your history, ready to reuse and refine.

Peachgen doesn't just produce a single great asset — it becomes a creative habit. As your content needs expand, the platform keeps up, adding new models, formats, and tools without ever asking you to learn design.

Every part of Peachgen works toward one promise: professional-quality media for everyone. It gives creators the power to express boldly while staying effortless to use — the result is a creative experience that feels both powerful and within reach. A Hithuc original.
